section.title: Rules for Christian Households
section.sectionRationale: The new life in Christ must reshape the most ordinary relationships, so Paul applies it to the first-century household: wives and husbands, children and fathers, slaves and masters. Each pair receives a word, and every instruction is anchored in the Lord, in the Lord, pleasing to the Lord, fearing the Lord, serving the Lord Christ. The longest word goes to slaves, whose humble work is to be done for Christ, and the code ends by reminding masters that they too answer to a Master in heaven. The gospel of chapter three now walks into the home.
section.thesis: The risen life of Christ transforms the household, so that wives and husbands, children and fathers, slaves and masters each live out their relationships as unto the Lord, working sincerely for Christ who shows no favoritism and rewards His servants, while masters remember they too have a Master in heaven.
section.frameNote: Three movements: wives and husbands, in mutual submission and love; children and fathers, in obedience and gentleness; and slaves and masters, the longest word, calling for wholehearted work as for the Lord Christ who judges without favoritism, and reminding masters of their own Master in heaven.

units.label: Wives and husbands
units.note: submission and love in the Lord

verses.text: Wives, {{hypotasso|submit}} to your husbands, as is fitting in the Lord.
Husbands, love your wives and do not be harsh with them.
Children, obey your parents in everything, for this is pleasing to the Lord.
Fathers, do not provoke your children, so they will not become discouraged.

units.label: Slaves and masters
units.note: working as for the Lord Christ, who shows no favoritism

verses.text: Slaves, obey your earthly masters in everything, not only to please them while they are watching, but with sincerity of heart and fear of the Lord.
Whatever you do, work at it with your whole being, as for the Lord and not for men,
because you know that you will receive an {{kleronomia|inheritance}} from the Lord as your reward. It is the Lord Christ you are serving.
Whoever does wrong will be repaid for his wrong, and there is no {{prosopolempsia|favoritism}}.
Masters, supply your slaves with what is right and fair, since you know that you also have a Master in heaven.

hypotasso.gloss: To arrange under, to submit or order oneself under another, originally a military term for ranking under an order. Wives are called to submit to their husbands as is fitting in the Lord, a voluntary ordering, not servility, that mirrors the church's response to Christ. It is set within a code where husbands are commanded to love, so authority is never a license for harshness.

kleronomia.gloss: An inheritance, a portion received as an heir. Strikingly, Paul promises the inheritance to slaves, who by Roman law could own and inherit nothing, telling them their true reward comes from the Lord. In Christ the lowest servant is a full heir of God, which utterly reframes their daily labor as service rendered to the Lord Christ.

prosopolempsia.gloss: Partiality or favoritism, literally the receiving of a face, judging by outward status. Paul says the Lord shows no favoritism: masters get no special treatment and wronged slaves are not overlooked. The same impartial Judge reckons with everyone, which both comforts the mistreated servant and warns the powerful master.

basic.context: The new life of chapter three now enters the home. Paul speaks to the three pairs that made up a first-century household: wives and husbands, children and fathers, slaves and masters. To each he gives a short, pointed word, and he keeps tying every duty to Christ: do this as is fitting in the Lord, pleasing to the Lord, out of fear of the Lord. He spends the most words on slaves, telling them to work wholeheartedly for Christ, who will reward them, and he reminds masters that they too must answer to a Master in heaven.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a household code, a familiar form in the ancient world, but Paul reshapes it around Christ and makes it strikingly mutual: husbands must love, fathers must not provoke, masters must be fair. The instructions to slaves describe how to live faithfully within an existing system; they are not an endorsement of slavery, and the whole New Testament trajectory undermines it. The abiding principles are Christ-centered relationships, love, and integrity.
basic.originalLanguages: Wives are to hypotasso, to order themselves under their husbands, a voluntary submission set beside the husband's command to love. Slaves are promised a kleronomia, an inheritance, though by law they could own nothing, because their true reward is from the Lord. And the Lord shows no prosopolempsia, no favoritism, judging master and slave alike by the same impartial standard.
basic.typology: The ordering of the Christian household reflects the greater relationship of Christ and His church, which Ephesians makes explicit. The inheritance promised to slaves fulfills the hope of God's people receiving their portion, now open to the lowest servant in Christ. The impartial Judge who shows no favoritism reflects God's own justice throughout Scripture.
basic.christ: Christ is named in nearly every line: relationships are ordered in the Lord, obedience pleases the Lord, work is done for the Lord Christ, and He is the one who gives the inheritance and judges without favoritism. Even a slave's daily task becomes direct service to Jesus. Christ is Lord of the whole household, high and low alike.
basic.god: The God revealed here is perfectly just and impartial, showing no favoritism to the powerful and not overlooking the wronged. He is the Master in heaven to whom even earthly masters must answer, and the Lord who rewards faithful service with an inheritance. He dignifies the humblest worker and holds the mightiest accountable.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 5 and 6 give a fuller version of this same household code, grounding marriage in Christ and the church. First Peter 2 and 3 apply similar instructions to servants and spouses. And Philemon shows Paul urging a master to receive his slave as a beloved brother.
basic.oneStory: The gospel that rescues and renews now reaches into ordinary homes, reordering the closest relationships under the lordship of Christ. In Him the powerless are made heirs and the powerful are humbled before their heavenly Master, a foretaste of the kingdom where the last are first and Christ is Lord of all.

context.literary: This is a household code, addressing the three standard pairs of the ancient home in turn, each with a duty and often a reason. It flows directly from 3:17, whatever you do, do it all in the name of the Lord Jesus, applying that principle to domestic life. The pattern of a shorter word to the one in authority and a fuller word elsewhere, here the extended address to slaves, reflects the pastoral realities of the Colossian church, which likely included the slave Onesimus.
context.historical: In the Greco-Roman world the household was the basic social unit, and its structure, including slavery, was taken for granted; moralists commonly wrote codes prescribing the duties of each member. Paul adopts the form but transforms it: he addresses wives, children, and slaves as responsible moral agents, commands love and fairness from those in power, and subjects everyone to Christ, which was countercultural in a hierarchy that favored the powerful.
context.canonical: This code parallels Ephesians 5:22 to 6:9 and stands alongside 1 Peter 2:18 to 3:7. The specific concern for slaves connects to the letter to Philemon, sent with Colossians, where Paul appeals for the slave Onesimus to be welcomed as a brother, showing the gospel's inner tension with the institution of slavery.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To apply the new life in Christ to everyday household relationships, calling each member to live under Christ's lordship, tempering all authority with love and fairness, dignifying the humblest worker as a servant of Christ and an heir, and reminding those in power that they answer to a heavenly Master.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The call to Christ-centered relationships, love, obedience, gentleness, integrity, and fairness, is prescriptive for believers. The specific household roles reflect the first-century social setting. The instructions to slaves describe faithful living within a system Paul does not thereby endorse; the wider biblical trajectory, seen in Philemon and in the equal dignity of all in Christ, points beyond slavery.

debates.question: How should the household code, especially the address to slaves, be applied today?

views.label: Enduring principle, culturally framed application
views.case: The abiding call is to Christlike love, respect, and integrity in relationships; the specific first-century roles, and above all the address to slaves, are framed by a social order Paul regulates but does not commend.

views.label: Regulating, not endorsing, slavery
views.case: By commanding masters to treat slaves justly and reminding them of their own Master, and by making slaves fellow heirs, Paul plants principles that undercut the institution, fully expressed in Philemon.

debates.bearing: Read rightly, the passage teaches Christ-centered, mutual, dignifying relationships, not a divine sanction of slavery or of harsh, one-sided authority.

study.originalLanguages: Wives are called to hypotasso, to order themselves under their husbands, a word of voluntary, ordered submission rather than forced servility, and it is immediately balanced by the husband's command to love and not be harsh, so authority is fenced in by love. The most arresting term falls to slaves: they are promised a kleronomia, an inheritance, though under Roman law a slave could own and inherit nothing, and Paul says their true reward comes from the Lord, and it is the Lord Christ they serve. Finally the code rests on the character of the Judge who shows no prosopolempsia, no favoritism, literally no receiving of a face by outward status, so the mistreated slave is not overlooked and the powerful master is not excused. The same impartial Lord reckons with all.

typology.fulfillment: Reflects the greater relationship of Christ and His church, made explicit in Ephesians 5, where marriage images the union of the Lord and His people.
Fulfills the hope of God's people receiving their portion, now opened even to the lowest servant, who in Christ becomes a full heir of God.
Reflects God's own justice, showing no favoritism, ultimately revealed in Christ who judges every person truly and without regard to status.

study.christ: Christ is the thread running through every instruction. Wives submit as is fitting in the Lord, children's obedience is pleasing to the Lord, slaves work with fear of the Lord and for the Lord Christ, and it is Christ who gives the inheritance and who judges without favoritism. The genius of the code is that it lifts the eyes of every household member, however high or low, to the same Lord: even scrubbing a floor becomes service rendered directly to Jesus. Christ is Lord of the home, dignifying the servant, restraining the master, and receiving the ordinary work of ordinary people as offered to Himself.
study.god: The God who stands behind this code is perfectly just and utterly impartial. He shows no favoritism to the powerful and does not overlook the wrongs done to the weak; the same standard measures master and slave. He is the Master in heaven to whom earthly masters must answer, which levels every human hierarchy under His authority. And He is the Lord who rewards faithful service with an inheritance, dignifying the humblest worker and holding the mightiest accountable. Here God is seen as the great equalizer, honoring the lowly and humbling the proud.

crossReferences.note: The fuller parallel household code, grounding marriage in the union of Christ and the church.
Similar instructions to servants and spouses, urging conduct that honors Christ before a watching world.
Paul urges a master to receive his runaway slave no longer as a slave but as a beloved brother.
In Christ there is neither slave nor free, the equality that reframes the whole household code.

study.oneStory: The gospel that rescues from darkness and renews the inner life now enters the front door of ordinary homes, reordering the closest human relationships under the lordship of Christ. In Him the powerless slave becomes a full heir, and the powerful master is humbled before his own heavenly Master. This is the kingdom breaking into daily life, a foretaste of the world to come, where the last are first, favoritism is abolished, and Christ is Lord of all.

quiz.q: How does Paul balance the instruction to wives with the instruction to husbands?

options.text: Wives are to submit as is fitting in the Lord, and husbands are to love and not be harsh.
options.note: Verses 18 and 19 pair submission with self-giving love, restraining authority.

options.text: Wives must obey while husbands may do as they please.
options.note: Husbands are explicitly commanded to love and not be harsh, not to please themselves.

options.text: Both are told simply to keep the peace at any cost.
options.note: Each receives a specific, Christ-centered duty, not vague peacekeeping.

options.text: Neither is given any responsibility to the other.
options.note: Both are given clear, mutual responsibilities under the Lord.

quiz.q: Why are fathers told not to provoke their children?

options.text: So the children will not become discouraged.
options.note: Verse 21 guards children's hearts from being crushed.

options.text: So the fathers will avoid legal trouble.
options.note: The concern is the child's spirit, not the father's reputation.

options.text: So the children will fear their fathers more.
options.note: The aim is encouragement, not fear.

options.text: So the fathers can avoid all responsibility.
options.note: Fathers are given active responsibility for gentle nurture.

quiz.q: How are slaves told to do their work?

options.text: Wholeheartedly, as for the Lord and not for men, with sincerity and fear of the Lord.
options.note: Verses 22 and 23 reframe daily labor as service to Christ.

options.text: Only when the master is watching.
options.note: Paul says not only to please them while watching but sincerely for the Lord.

options.text: As little as they can get away with.
options.note: They are to work with their whole being, as for the Lord.

options.text: With resentment, since the work is beneath them.
options.note: They serve the Lord Christ, which dignifies even humble work.

quiz.q: What reward does Paul promise faithful slaves, and why is it striking?

options.text: An inheritance from the Lord, striking because slaves could legally own nothing.
options.note: Verse 24 makes the lowest servant a full heir in Christ.

options.text: Immediate freedom from their masters.
options.note: The promise is a heavenly inheritance, not automatic manumission.

options.text: Higher social rank in the community.
options.note: The reward is from the Lord, not social advancement.

options.text: Nothing; their work goes unnoticed.
options.note: Their work is seen and rewarded by the Lord Christ.

quiz.q: What does Paul remind masters of in 4:1?

options.text: That they must be right and fair, since they also have a Master in heaven.
options.note: Masters answer to their own heavenly Master, which curbs abuse.

options.text: That they may treat slaves however they wish.
options.note: They are commanded to be just and fair, not to do as they please.

options.text: That they are accountable to no one.
options.note: They are accountable to the Master in heaven.

options.text: That earthly power is all that matters.
options.note: Their earthly power is placed under God's higher authority.

questions: Paul ties every household duty to the Lord. How would consciously doing your family or work relationships for Christ change the way you treat the people closest to you?
Authority in this passage is always fenced in by love and fairness. Where do you hold some authority, and how could you exercise it with more gentleness and justice?
Slaves were told their ordinary work was service to the Lord Christ. What routine or unnoticed task could you begin to offer to Jesus as worship this week?
God shows no favoritism, honoring the lowly and holding the powerful accountable. How does that truth comfort you if you feel overlooked, or challenge you where you hold power?
Even a master answers to a Master in heaven. Who has been entrusted to your care, and how does remembering your own accountability to God shape how you lead them?

translationNotes.note: Submit renders hypotasso, a voluntary ordering of oneself under another, set within a mutual code that commands husbands to love; it does not describe servility or excuse harshness.
Earthly masters translates masters according to the flesh, distinguishing human masters from the one Lord in heaven whom all ultimately serve.
Inheritance is striking because Roman slaves could not legally inherit; Paul declares that in Christ the faithful slave receives the inheritance from the Lord as reward.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Colossians 3:18-4:1. No em or en dashes appear in this passage.
